image.title: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Electrochemical Halocyclization‐Enabled Synthesis of Tetrahydrofuran Derivatives With KBr/ KI
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A mild electrochemical strategy has been developed for synthesizing multisubstituted tetrahydrofuran derivatives via 1,5‐halocyclization of unactivated alkenols. This method employs readily available KBr/ KI as both the halogen source and supporting electrolyte, requiring no external oxidants or metal catalysts and producing H2 as the sole byproduct. This oxidant‐ and catalyst‐free protocol provides an efficient, atom‐economical route to synthetically important tetrahydrofuran (THF) scaffolds.
Tetrahydrofuran (THF) frameworks are privileged scaffolds prevalent in bioactive natural products and pharmaceuticals, yet the stereocontrolled synthesis of polysubstituted derivatives remains a significant challenge. While conventional methods often rely on transition‐metal catalysis, they are frequently hampered by issues such as β‐hydride elimination and the requirement for stoichiometric oxidants. In contrast, organic electrosynthesis offers a sustainable alternative by using electron transfer to activate substrates, eliminating the need for external oxidants and improving atom economy. Building on recent advances in electrochemical halogenation, this method proposes a novel and efficient strategy for constructing THF rings. We envisioned an electrochemical 1,5‐halocyclization of alkenols using inexpensive metal halides as dual‐function halogen sources and electrolytes. This anode‐driven process enables rapid assembly of the THF core, producing molecular hydrogen as the sole byproduct. This method represents a green, atom‐economical, and operationally simple approach to valuable O‐heterocycles.
item.content:encoded:
A mild electrochemical strategy has been developed for synthesizing multisubstituted tetrahydrofuran derivatives via 1,5-halocyclization of unactivated alkenols. This method employs readily available KBr/ KI as both the halogen source and supporting electrolyte, requiring no external oxidants or metal catalysts and producing H2 as the sole byproduct. This oxidant- and catalyst-free protocol provides an efficient, atom-economical route to synthetically important tetrahydrofuran (THF) scaffolds.


Tetrahydrofuran (THF) frameworks are privileged scaffolds prevalent in bioactive natural products and pharmaceuticals, yet the stereocontrolled synthesis of polysubstituted derivatives remains a significant challenge. While conventional methods often rely on transition-metal catalysis, they are frequently hampered by issues such as β-hydride elimination and the requirement for stoichiometric oxidants. In contrast, organic electrosynthesis offers a sustainable alternative by using electron transfer to activate substrates, eliminating the need for external oxidants and improving atom economy. Building on recent advances in electrochemical halogenation, this method proposes a novel and efficient strategy for constructing THF rings. We envisioned an electrochemical 1,5-halocyclization of alkenols using inexpensive metal halides as dual-function halogen sources and electrolytes. This anode-driven process enables rapid assembly of the THF core, producing molecular hydrogen as the sole byproduct. This method represents a green, atom-economical, and operationally simple approach to valuable O-heterocycles.
item.dc:creator: Zhenlei Zou, Jie Dong, Hui Wang, Tianle Xia, Zhaopan Yang, Weigang Zhang, Yi Wang
item.dc:title: Electrochemical Halocyclization‐Enabled Synthesis of Tetrahydrofuran Derivatives With KBr/ KI

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Advances in Transition‐Metal‐ and External Photocatalyst‐Free Photochemical Reactions
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
This review summarizes recent advances in photochemical reactions that proceed without transition metals or external photocatalysts. By employing either direct excitation of substrates or photoexcitation of in situ formed electron donor–acceptor complexes, these transformations proceed via energy‐transfer, single‐electron transfer, or hydrogen atom transfer pathways, enabling efficient construction of C–C and C–heteroatom bonds under mild conditions.
In recent years, transition‐metal‐ and external photocatalyst‐free photochemical reactions have garnered significant attention as environmentally friendly and sustainable approaches to organic synthesis. These reactions rely on either direct photoexcitation of substrates or photoexcitation of electron donor–acceptor complexes to enable diverse transformations. This review summarizes representative advances reported in the recent years, organized by the construction of C–C and C–heteroatom bonds, and highlights key mechanistic features. This review aims to provide guidance and inspiration for reaction design and mechanistic exploration in this field.
item.content:encoded:
This review summarizes recent advances in photochemical reactions that proceed without transition metals or external photocatalysts. By employing either direct excitation of substrates or photoexcitation of in situ formed electron donor–acceptor complexes, these transformations proceed via energy-transfer, single-electron transfer, or hydrogen atom transfer pathways, enabling efficient construction of C–C and C–heteroatom bonds under mild conditions.


In recent years, transition-metal- and external photocatalyst-free photochemical reactions have garnered significant attention as environmentally friendly and sustainable approaches to organic synthesis. These reactions rely on either direct photoexcitation of substrates or photoexcitation of electron donor–acceptor complexes to enable diverse transformations. This review summarizes representative advances reported in the recent years, organized by the construction of C–C and C–heteroatom bonds, and highlights key mechanistic features. This review aims to provide guidance and inspiration for reaction design and mechanistic exploration in this field.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis of Novel C‐Acyl‐Glycosyl Sulfonamides via Pd‐Catalyzed Carbonylative Coupling
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A Pd‐catalyzed carbonylative coupling of 1‐iodo‐glycals with diverse sulfonamides using Mo(CO)6 enables efficient access to C‐acyl‐glycosyl sulfonamides.This robust and versatile strategy delivers novel glycoside derivatives in good to excellent yields, highlighting their potential for drug discovery.
We developed an efficient methodology for synthesizing C‐acyl‐glycosyl sulfonamides via a carbonylative coupling reaction, using 1‐iodo‐glycals as substrates. A wide range of aromatic, heteroaromatic, and aliphatic sulfonamides was explored, employing Mo(CO)6 as a safe, solid source of carbon monoxide gas. This approach enabled the preparation of novel glycosyl sulfonamides featuring a C‐acyl‐linked framework under mild reaction conditions, achieving good to excellent yields.
item.content:encoded:
A Pd-catalyzed carbonylative coupling of 1-iodo-glycals with diverse sulfonamides using Mo(CO)6 enables efficient access to C-acyl-glycosyl sulfonamides.This robust and versatile strategy delivers novel glycoside derivatives in good to excellent yields, highlighting their potential for drug discovery.


We developed an efficient methodology for synthesizing C-acyl-glycosyl sulfonamides via a carbonylative coupling reaction, using 1-iodo-glycals as substrates. A wide range of aromatic, heteroaromatic, and aliphatic sulfonamides was explored, employing Mo(CO)6 as a safe, solid source of carbon monoxide gas. This approach enabled the preparation of novel glycosyl sulfonamides featuring a C-acyl-linked framework under mild reaction conditions, achieving good to excellent yields.
item.dc:creator: João A. F. Silva, Mônica F. Z. J. Toledo, Flávia Manarin, João H. de Araujo‐Neto, Milene M. Hornink, Hélio A. Stefani
item.dc:title: Synthesis of Novel C‐Acyl‐Glycosyl Sulfonamides via Pd‐Catalyzed Carbonylative Coupling

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Advances in the Synthesis of Chromeno[4,3‐b]pyrrolidines
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Chromeno[4,3‐b]pyrrolidine derivatives are a class of compounds featuring a unique tricyclic fused scaffold and exhibiting significant biological activities. This review systematically summarizes the cyclization reactions catalyzed by various catalysts, all of which are employed for the construction of the Chromeno[4,3‐b]pyrrolidine scaffold.
Chromeno[4,3‐b]pyrrolidine derivatives are a class of compounds featuring a unique tricyclic fused scaffold and exhibiting significant biological activities. In recent years, remarkable progress has been made in developing efficient synthetic strategies for these complex structures. This review aims to systematically summarize various methods for synthesizing chromeno[4,3‐b]pyrrolidine derivatives. Based on the type of catalyst employed, we have summarized and categorized four main catalytic strategies: Brønsted base catalysis, N‐heterocyclic carbene catalysis, phosphine catalysis, and metal catalysis. The cyclization strategies and their proposed reaction mechanisms are discussed in detail, and we believe this review will provide valuable inspiration for future innovative research in this field.
item.content:encoded:
Chromeno[4,3-b]pyrrolidine derivatives are a class of compounds featuring a unique tricyclic fused scaffold and exhibiting significant biological activities. This review systematically summarizes the cyclization reactions catalyzed by various catalysts, all of which are employed for the construction of the Chromeno[4,3-b]pyrrolidine scaffold.


Chromeno[4,3-b]pyrrolidine derivatives are a class of compounds featuring a unique tricyclic fused scaffold and exhibiting significant biological activities. In recent years, remarkable progress has been made in developing efficient synthetic strategies for these complex structures. This review aims to systematically summarize various methods for synthesizing chromeno[4,3-b]pyrrolidine derivatives. Based on the type of catalyst employed, we have summarized and categorized four main catalytic strategies: Brønsted base catalysis, N-heterocyclic carbene catalysis, phosphine catalysis, and metal catalysis. The cyclization strategies and their proposed reaction mechanisms are discussed in detail, and we believe this review will provide valuable inspiration for future innovative research in this field.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Photoinduced Halogen Atom Transfer of Alkyl Halides With N‐Arylsulfenyl Phthalimides Toward Alkyl‐Aryl Sulfides
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
An operationally simple photocatalytic strategy has been developed for the direct activation of alkyl halides via halogen atom transfer, enabling their coupling with N‐arylsulfenyl phthalimides to afford alkyl‐aryl sulfides. This transition‐metal‐free protocol proceeds under mild conditions and demonstrates a broad substrate scope.
The sulfenylation of organohalides has become a powerful strategy for CS bond formation. However, methods for activating alkyl halides in this context remain scarce. Herein, we report a simple photocatalytic strategy that generates alkyl radicals from alkyl halides via halogen atom transfer. These radicals subsequently couple efficiently with N‐arylsulfenyl phthalimides to afford alkyl‐aryl sulfides. This approach is transition‐metal‐free, operates under mild conditions, and exhibits a broad substrate scope.
item.content:encoded:
An operationally simple photocatalytic strategy has been developed for the direct activation of alkyl halides via halogen atom transfer, enabling their coupling with N-arylsulfenyl phthalimides to afford alkyl-aryl sulfides. This transition-metal-free protocol proceeds under mild conditions and demonstrates a broad substrate scope.


The sulfenylation of organohalides has become a powerful strategy for CS bond formation. However, methods for activating alkyl halides in this context remain scarce. Herein, we report a simple photocatalytic strategy that generates alkyl radicals from alkyl halides via halogen atom transfer. These radicals subsequently couple efficiently with N-arylsulfenyl phthalimides to afford alkyl-aryl sulfides. This approach is transition-metal-free, operates under mild conditions, and exhibits a broad substrate scope.
item.dc:creator: Xin Yang, Maojian Lu, Ting-Ting Miao, Fang Ye, Qian Xiao, Qing‐Xiao Tong, Jian‐Ji Zhong
item.dc:title: Photoinduced Halogen Atom Transfer of Alkyl Halides With N‐Arylsulfenyl Phthalimides Toward Alkyl‐Aryl Sulfides

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: [3 + 2] Cycloaddition of (Diazomethyl)dialkylphosphine Oxide and Aryldiazonium Salts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 17, 5 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
Silver‐catalyzed [3 + 2] cycloaddition of aryldiazonium salts and (diazomethyl)dimethylphosphine oxide is developed as an approach to 2,5‐disubstituted tetrazoles bearing a P(O)(Alkyl)2 group. The method has wide scope and low susceptibility toward electronic and steric effects in the diazonium salt component.
A convenient approach to 2,5‐disubstituted tetrazoles bearing a dialkylphosphine oxide substituent at the C‐5 position is developed. The method involves silver‐catalyzed [3 + 2] cycloaddition of (diazomethyl)dialkylphosphine oxide and aryl diazonium salts. The proposed reaction is compatible with various substituents at the aryl ring and has low susceptibility to their electronic and steric effects. Several heterocyclic diazonium salts were also involved into the transformation successfully. The proposed reaction pathway may include synchronous [3 + 2] cycloaddition or two‐step formation of the tetrazole ring starting with electrophilic attack of aryl diazonium salt at the diazoalkane carbon atom.
item.content:encoded:
Silver-catalyzed [3 + 2] cycloaddition of aryldiazonium salts and (diazomethyl)dimethylphosphine oxide is developed as an approach to 2,5-disubstituted tetrazoles bearing a P(O)(Alkyl)2 group. The method has wide scope and low susceptibility toward electronic and steric effects in the diazonium salt component.


A convenient approach to 2,5-disubstituted tetrazoles bearing a dialkylphosphine oxide substituent at the C-5 position is developed. The method involves silver-catalyzed [3 + 2] cycloaddition of (diazomethyl)dialkylphosphine oxide and aryl diazonium salts. The proposed reaction is compatible with various substituents at the aryl ring and has low susceptibility to their electronic and steric effects. Several heterocyclic diazonium salts were also involved into the transformation successfully. The proposed reaction pathway may include synchronous [3 + 2] cycloaddition or two-step formation of the tetrazole ring starting with electrophilic attack of aryl diazonium salt at the diazoalkane carbon atom.
item.dc:creator: Ilona Tarasiuk, Evgeniy Y. Slobodyanyuk, Dmytro O. Sibgatulin, Oleksandr O. Grygorenko
item.dc:title: [3 + 2] Cycloaddition of (Diazomethyl)dialkylphosphine Oxide and Aryldiazonium Salts

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthesis and Reactivity of Functionalized Tetrazenes: An Original Route Toward 1,2,3‐Triazoles
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 17, 5 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
A series of functionalized tetrazenes was synthesized as N‐rich compounds featuring both modular architecture and versatile reactivity. Thanks to various terminal groups, these derivatives could be used for accessing higher polynitrogenated structures such as tetrazenic oligomers or polymers. Harnessing β‐activated tetrazenes led to a novel synthetic route for accessing 1,2,3‐triazoles.
New alkyltetrazene structures with various terminal functional groups (hydroxyl, halogen, amine, hydrazine) basically in β‐position were synthesized through the oxidation of their hydrazine precursors as novel ingredients for accessing nitrogen‐densified structures. To synthesize hydrazine‐ and amine‐substituted alkyltetrazenes, synthetic routes involving protection and deprotection steps were designed. In addition, β‐activated alkyltetrazenes were obtained from their diol precursors, and their reactivity was investigated, resulting in a novel synthetic route of 1,2,3‐triazoles according to two complementary approaches. The scope was extended to β‐disubstituted derivatives, whose syntheses were also fully described.
item.content:encoded:
A series of functionalized tetrazenes was synthesized as N-rich compounds featuring both modular architecture and versatile reactivity. Thanks to various terminal groups, these derivatives could be used for accessing higher polynitrogenated structures such as tetrazenic oligomers or polymers. Harnessing β-activated tetrazenes led to a novel synthetic route for accessing 1,2,3-triazoles.


New alkyltetrazene structures with various terminal functional groups (hydroxyl, halogen, amine, hydrazine) basically in β-position were synthesized through the oxidation of their hydrazine precursors as novel ingredients for accessing nitrogen-densified structures. To synthesize hydrazine- and amine-substituted alkyltetrazenes, synthetic routes involving protection and deprotection steps were designed. In addition, β-activated alkyltetrazenes were obtained from their diol precursors, and their reactivity was investigated, resulting in a novel synthetic route of 1,2,3-triazoles according to two complementary approaches. The scope was extended to β-disubstituted derivatives, whose syntheses were also fully described.
item.dc:creator: Louis Le Moigne, John Eymann, Anne Renault, Emmanuel Lacôte, Lionel Joucla, Chaza Darwich
item.dc:title: Synthesis and Reactivity of Functionalized Tetrazenes: An Original Route Toward 1,2,3‐Triazoles

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Enantioselective Synthesis of Ferrocene‐Fused Planar‐Chiral Cyclic Phosphonic Acid Derivatives
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 17, 5 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
The asymmetric synthesis of planar‐chiral ferrocene‐fused cyclic phosphonic acids (S)‐1 was achieved starting with chiral acetals (–)‐2. Enantioselective introduction of phosphoryl and 2‐hydroxyethyl groups in (S)‐6 followed by cyclization/hydrolysis provided (S)‐1 in enantiomerically pure forms. The structure of (S)‐1 was determined by X‐ray crystallography, and the application of Brønsted acidic (S)‐1a as chiral organocatalysts was examined.
Planar‐chiral ferrocene‐fused cyclic phosphonic acid half‐ester (S)‐1a was prepared in an enantiomerically pure form by the enantioselective transformation. The synthesis started from Kagan's chiral ferrocenyl acetal (–)‐2a and resulted in ferrocene derivative (S)‐6a substituted by a 2‐hydroethyl substituent at the 1‐position and by a (EtO)2P(O)‐ group at the 2‐position. The chiral acetal substituent in (–)‐2a is a powerful chiral directing group, and thus planar‐chiral (S)‐6a was isolated in >99% ee. The base‐promoted intramolecular transesterification/cyclization of (S)‐6 and following selective hydrolysis of the exocyclic ethoxy moiety afforded (S)‐1a in an enantiomerically pure form. The (η5‐C5Me5)Fe and (η5‐C5Ph5)Fe analogues, (S)‐1b and (S)‐1c, were prepared by the similar strategy, but the use of the exocyclic benzyl ester and the palladium‐catalyzed benzyl‐selective hydrogenolysis is essential to complete the synthesis of (S)‐1b/(S)‐1c. Brønsted acidic (S)‐1a‐c were examined as chiral organocatalysts in the direct Mannich reaction between N‐boc‐imine 10 and acetylacetone showing good catalytic activity and modest enantioselectivity.
item.content:encoded:
The asymmetric synthesis of planar-chiral ferrocene-fused cyclic phosphonic acids (S)-1 was achieved starting with chiral acetals (–)-2. Enantioselective introduction of phosphoryl and 2-hydroxyethyl groups in (S)-6 followed by cyclization/hydrolysis provided (S)-1 in enantiomerically pure forms. The structure of (S)-1 was determined by X-ray crystallography, and the application of Brønsted acidic (S)-1a as chiral organocatalysts was examined.


Planar-chiral ferrocene-fused cyclic phosphonic acid half-ester (S)-1a was prepared in an enantiomerically pure form by the enantioselective transformation. The synthesis started from Kagan's chiral ferrocenyl acetal (–)-2a and resulted in ferrocene derivative (S)-6a substituted by a 2-hydroethyl substituent at the 1-position and by a (EtO)2P(O)- group at the 2-position. The chiral acetal substituent in (–)-2a is a powerful chiral directing group, and thus planar-chiral (S)-6a was isolated in >99% ee. The base-promoted intramolecular transesterification/cyclization of (S)-6 and following selective hydrolysis of the exocyclic ethoxy moiety afforded (S)-1a in an enantiomerically pure form. The (η 5-C5Me5)Fe and (η 5-C5Ph5)Fe analogues, (S)-1b and (S)-1c, were prepared by the similar strategy, but the use of the exocyclic benzyl ester and the palladium-catalyzed benzyl-selective hydrogenolysis is essential to complete the synthesis of (S)-1b/(S)-1c. Brønsted acidic (S)-1a-c were examined as chiral organocatalysts in the direct Mannich reaction between N-boc-imine 10 and acetylacetone showing good catalytic activity and modest enantioselectivity.
item.dc:creator: Takehito Ohji, Akinoshin Okazaki, Qiang Liu, Risa Yasue, Kazuhiro Yoshida, Masamichi Ogasawara
item.dc:title: Enantioselective Synthesis of Ferrocene‐Fused Planar‐Chiral Cyclic Phosphonic Acid Derivatives

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Investigating the Reactivity and Selectivity in Nucleophilic Fluorination of Secondary Alkyl Bromides and Mesylates: Assessment of the Effect of Counterion and Bulky Alcohols
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 17, 5 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
Hydrogen bonding provided by pinacol or 2‐trifluoromethyl‐2‐propanol enhances the selectivity of nucleophilic fluorination of several secondary alkyl bromides or mesylates.
Synthesis of secondary alkyl fluorides via nucleophilic substitution faces the problem of high competition with E2 processes. This competition depends on the chemical environment of the substrate, the counterion, and the hydrogen bonding to the fluoride ion. Herein, we combined reliable theoretical calculations and experimental studies to probe the reactivity and selectivity of different substrates. The calculations have shown the significance of SN2 versus E2 competition in various chemical environments. In the experiments, we have tested the use of potassium fluoride combined with 18‐crown‐6 and the bulky alcohols pinacol and 2‐trifluoromethyl‐2‐propanol (TBOH‐F3) with several alkyl bromides and mesylates. The combination of tetrabutylammonium fluoride (TBAF) with these alcohols was also evaluated. We have found that potassium fluoride, combined with the crown ether and the bulky alcohols, is more selective than TBAF combined with these alcohols, although TBAF is more reactive. Alkyl mesylates are less prone to the formation of E2 products, and high fluorination yields can be obtained when using KF (potassium fluoride) combined with 18‐crown‐6 and pinacol in acetonitrile solvent. We have also identified 2‐bromo‐1‐phenylpropane as a very challenging substrate. Even when using a high concentration of 1,1,1,3,3,3‐hexafluoro‐2‐propanol in acetonitrile solution, fluorination of this substrate was not achieved.
item.content:encoded:
Hydrogen bonding provided by pinacol or 2-trifluoromethyl-2-propanol enhances the selectivity of nucleophilic fluorination of several secondary alkyl bromides or mesylates.


Synthesis of secondary alkyl fluorides via nucleophilic substitution faces the problem of high competition with E2 processes. This competition depends on the chemical environment of the substrate, the counterion, and the hydrogen bonding to the fluoride ion. Herein, we combined reliable theoretical calculations and experimental studies to probe the reactivity and selectivity of different substrates. The calculations have shown the significance of S N 2 versus E2 competition in various chemical environments. In the experiments, we have tested the use of potassium fluoride combined with 18-crown-6 and the bulky alcohols pinacol and 2-trifluoromethyl-2-propanol (TBOH-F3) with several alkyl bromides and mesylates. The combination of tetrabutylammonium fluoride (TBAF) with these alcohols was also evaluated. We have found that potassium fluoride, combined with the crown ether and the bulky alcohols, is more selective than TBAF combined with these alcohols, although TBAF is more reactive. Alkyl mesylates are less prone to the formation of E2 products, and high fluorination yields can be obtained when using KF (potassium fluoride) combined with 18-crown-6 and pinacol in acetonitrile solvent. We have also identified 2-bromo-1-phenylpropane as a very challenging substrate. Even when using a high concentration of 1,1,1,3,3,3-hexafluoro-2-propanol in acetonitrile solution, fluorination of this substrate was not achieved.
item.dc:creator: Eloah P. Ávila, Luis F. Resende, Mauro V. de Almeida, Josefredo R. Pliego
item.dc:title: Investigating the Reactivity and Selectivity in Nucleophilic Fluorination of Secondary Alkyl Bromides and Mesylates: Assessment of the Effect of Counterion and Bulky Alcohols

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Direct Amidation of 2‐Methylquinolines With Anilines Under Transition‐Metal‐Free Conditions Using Air as the Sole Oxidant
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 17, 5 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
A facile aerobic oxidative coupling of 2‐methylquinolines with anilines has been developed, which employs air as the sole oxidant to afford aromatic amides under transition‐metal‐free conditions. With potassium tert‐butoxide as the promoter, this direct amidation proceeds smoothly at a lower reaction temperature (75°C) compared with previously reported protocols (120°C–130°C).
A facile oxidative coupling of 2‐methylquinolines with anilines that uses air as the sole oxidant is described. The reaction affords a straightforward route to aromatic amides under transition‐metal‐free conditions. With potassium tert‐butoxide as the promoter, the direct amidation reaction proceeds smoothly at a lower reaction temperature relative to reported protocols (75°C vs. 120°C–130°C). Notably, the replacement of 2‐methylquinoline with 2‐quinolinecarboxaldehyde, a putative intermediate in previous studies, for the coupling with aniline resulted in a markedly lower yield of the target aromatic amide (59% vs. 84%). This unexpected finding offers new possible considerations for relevant research in organic chemistry.
item.content:encoded:
A facile aerobic oxidative coupling of 2-methylquinolines with anilines has been developed, which employs air as the sole oxidant to afford aromatic amides under transition-metal-free conditions. With potassium tert-butoxide as the promoter, this direct amidation proceeds smoothly at a lower reaction temperature (75°C) compared with previously reported protocols (120°C–130°C).


A facile oxidative coupling of 2-methylquinolines with anilines that uses air as the sole oxidant is described. The reaction affords a straightforward route to aromatic amides under transition-metal-free conditions. With potassium tert-butoxide as the promoter, the direct amidation reaction proceeds smoothly at a lower reaction temperature relative to reported protocols (75°C vs. 120°C–130°C). Notably, the replacement of 2-methylquinoline with 2-quinolinecarboxaldehyde, a putative intermediate in previous studies, for the coupling with aniline resulted in a markedly lower yield of the target aromatic amide (59% vs. 84%). This unexpected finding offers new possible considerations for relevant research in organic chemistry.
item.dc:creator: Jian‐Cai Chen, Lian‐Feng Ma, Hui‐Jing Li, Yan‐Chao Wu
item.dc:title: Direct Amidation of 2‐Methylquinolines With Anilines Under Transition‐Metal‐Free Conditions Using Air as the Sole Oxidant

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Elucidation of the Conformational Rate Processes of the Macrocyclic Lactone (–)‐Nonenolide Through Variable Temperature NMR, Quantum Mechanics, and Molecular Dynamics
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 17, 5 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
The conformational rate processes that influence the NMR spectra of the ten‐membered lactone (–)‐nonenolide are elucidated using VT‐NMR, QM calculations and MD simulations. Reorientation of the alkene moiety with respect to the ring is identified as the slow exchange process. Satisfactory chemical shift predictions required a molecular dynamics‐based analysis of the conformer populations in solution.
Variable‐temperature NMR spectra of the ten‐membered lactone (–)‐nonenolide reveal that the molecule undergoes a hindered conformational interconversion process, which appears to be characteristic of this class of natural products. Using a combination of quantum mechanics (QM) calculations and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, we show that the hindered rate process involves a ‘jump‐rope rotation’ that reorients the endocyclic alkene moiety with respect to the rest of the ring. Quantum mechanical calculations provide reasonably accurate predictions of the interconverting species’ chemical shifts, but several discrepancies exist, which we trace to inadequacies in the description of conformer energies in solution. Closer agreement is obtained from an MD‐based analysis of the conformer populations in explicit solvent. This study illustrates some important ways in which solvent effects can influence the accuracy of theoretical chemical shift predictions.
item.content:encoded:
The conformational rate processes that influence the NMR spectra of the ten-membered lactone (–)-nonenolide are elucidated using VT-NMR, QM calculations and MD simulations. Reorientation of the alkene moiety with respect to the ring is identified as the slow exchange process. Satisfactory chemical shift predictions required a molecular dynamics-based analysis of the conformer populations in solution.


Variable-temperature NMR spectra of the ten-membered lactone (–)-nonenolide reveal that the molecule undergoes a hindered conformational interconversion process, which appears to be characteristic of this class of natural products. Using a combination of quantum mechanics (QM) calculations and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, we show that the hindered rate process involves a ‘jump-rope rotation’ that reorients the endocyclic alkene moiety with respect to the rest of the ring. Quantum mechanical calculations provide reasonably accurate predictions of the interconverting species’ chemical shifts, but several discrepancies exist, which we trace to inadequacies in the description of conformer energies in solution. Closer agreement is obtained from an MD-based analysis of the conformer populations in explicit solvent. This study illustrates some important ways in which solvent effects can influence the accuracy of theoretical chemical shift predictions.
item.dc:creator: Ingso Limbu, Liam R. Hunt, Daniel P. Furkert, Margaret A. Brimble, Mary J. Garson, Elizabeth H. Krenske
item.dc:title: Elucidation of the Conformational Rate Processes of the Macrocyclic Lactone (–)‐Nonenolide Through Variable Temperature NMR, Quantum Mechanics, and Molecular Dynamics

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Huisgen Rearrangement as a Strategic Tool for Trifluoromethyl‐Integrated Energetic Materials
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 17, 5 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
Trifluoromethyl‐substituted energetic materials are developed by integrating C–C bonded 1,2,3‐triazoles with oxadiazoles via Huisgen cycloaddition and other facile routes. The compounds exhibit high densities, notable thermal stability, and low mechanical sensitivity. Experimental heats of formation and performance calculations reveal compound 10 as a promising high‐performing candidate, highlighting an effective design strategy beyond traditional CHNO energetic frameworks.
A series of trifluoromethyl‐substituted energetic materials were synthesized by combining CC bonded 1,2,3‐triazoles with 1,3,4‐ and 1,2,4‐oxadiazoles through the Huisgen cycloaddition and other facile synthetic routes. All the novel compounds were fully characterized by multinuclear NMR, IR, TGA‐DSC, and elemental analysis. Compound 10 was confirmed by 15N NMR spectroscopy, 6 and 11 were confirmed by single‐crystal XRD studies. Both compounds, 6 and 10, exhibit good densities (1.89 and 1.77 g cm−3), thermal stabilities (215 and 157°C), and are insensitive to mechanical stimuli (IS > 40 J, FS > 360 N). The solid‐state heat of formation was experimentally measured by an oxygen bomb calorimeter. Remarkably, compound 10 exhibits excellent detonation performance (VOD = 7308 m s−1, DP = 22.15 GPa), estimated through the EXPLO 5 software, outperforming previously reported trifluoromethyl‐substituted energetic materials to the best of our knowledge. Overall, this work underscores a balanced design strategy and an effective synthetic route for tuning energetic properties, marking progress beyond traditional CHNO‐based frameworks.
item.content:encoded:
Trifluoromethyl-substituted energetic materials are developed by integrating C–C bonded 1,2,3-triazoles with oxadiazoles via Huisgen cycloaddition and other facile routes. The compounds exhibit high densities, notable thermal stability, and low mechanical sensitivity. Experimental heats of formation and performance calculations reveal compound 10 as a promising high-performing candidate, highlighting an effective design strategy beyond traditional CHNO energetic frameworks.


A series of trifluoromethyl-substituted energetic materials were synthesized by combining CC bonded 1,2,3-triazoles with 1,3,4- and 1,2,4-oxadiazoles through the Huisgen cycloaddition and other facile synthetic routes. All the novel compounds were fully characterized by multinuclear NMR, IR, TGA-DSC, and elemental analysis. Compound 10 was confirmed by 15N NMR spectroscopy, 6 and 11 were confirmed by single-crystal XRD studies. Both compounds, 6 and 10, exhibit good densities (1.89 and 1.77 g cm−3), thermal stabilities (215 and 157°C), and are insensitive to mechanical stimuli (IS > 40 J, FS > 360 N). The solid-state heat of formation was experimentally measured by an oxygen bomb calorimeter. Remarkably, compound 10 exhibits excellent detonation performance (VOD = 7308 m s−1, DP = 22.15 GPa), estimated through the EXPLO 5 software, outperforming previously reported trifluoromethyl-substituted energetic materials to the best of our knowledge. Overall, this work underscores a balanced design strategy and an effective synthetic route for tuning energetic properties, marking progress beyond traditional CHNO-based frameworks.

item.dc:title: Huisgen Rearrangement as a Strategic Tool for Trifluoromethyl‐Integrated Energetic Materials

item.title: Front Cover: Synthetic and Medicinal Chemistry Perspectives on Cereblon‐Binding Molecular Glue Degraders: Recent Advances and Emerging Trends (Eur. J. Org. Chem. 17/2026)
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 17, 5 May 2026.
item.dc:description: The Front Cover highlights advances in CRBN‐recruiting molecular glue degraders since 2023, illustrating the shift from canonical glues to non‐canonical chemotypes that expand the neosubstrate space. A glutarimide anchor binds to CRBN, while ligand‐specific features shape the CRBN–ligand–neosubstrate interface. AI‐enabled modeling can further accelerate the discovery of atypical MGDs. More information can be found in the Review by N.‐J. Kim and co‐workers (DOI: 10.1002/ejoc.202501208). Created in BioRender: Won, H.‐J. (2026) https://BioRender.com/x39o4mt.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Synthetic and Medicinal Chemistry Perspectives on Cereblon‐Binding Molecular Glue Degraders: Recent Advances and Emerging Trends
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 17, 5 May 2026.
item.dc:description: This review highlights glutarimide‐based cereblon molecular glue degraders reported since 2023, focusing on their discovery strategies, synthetic approaches, SAR features, pharmacological activities, and neosubstrate recognition mechanisms.For the past decade, targeted protein degradation (TPD) has emerged as a transformative paradigm in medicinal chemistry and chemical biology. Among the various TPD approaches, cereblon (CRBN)‐recruiting molecular glue degraders (MGDs) have attracted considerable attention due to their favorable drug‐like properties and demonstrated clinical potential. This review provides an overview of recent advances since 2023 in the development of CRBN‐targeting MGDs from synthetic and medicinal chemistry perspectives. We highlight the evolution of novel chemotypes, synthetic strategies, structure–activity relationships (SARs), pharmacological activities, and the mechanistic understanding of ternary complex formation. Finally, we discuss emerging trends and future directions for the discovery and optimization of next‐generation molecular glues.
item.content:encoded:
This review highlights glutarimide-based cereblon molecular glue degraders reported since 2023, focusing on their discovery strategies, synthetic approaches, SAR features, pharmacological activities, and neosubstrate recognition mechanisms.


For the past decade, targeted protein degradation (TPD) has emerged as a transformative paradigm in medicinal chemistry and chemical biology. Among the various TPD approaches, cereblon (CRBN)-recruiting molecular glue degraders (MGDs) have attracted considerable attention due to their favorable drug-like properties and demonstrated clinical potential. This review provides an overview of recent advances since 2023 in the development of CRBN-targeting MGDs from synthetic and medicinal chemistry perspectives. We highlight the evolution of novel chemotypes, synthetic strategies, structure–activity relationships (SARs), pharmacological activities, and the mechanistic understanding of ternary complex formation. Finally, we discuss emerging trends and future directions for the discovery and optimization of next-generation molecular glues.
item.dc:creator: Hyuck‐Jae Won, Seung Mok Baek, Min Hoo Lee, Jae Hyeon Park, Jeong‐Won Shin, Yo‐Sep Yang, Yoon Hu Jang, Kyung‐Soo Inn, Nam‐Jung Kim
item.dc:title: Synthetic and Medicinal Chemistry Perspectives on Cereblon‐Binding Molecular Glue Degraders: Recent Advances and Emerging Trends

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Dearomatization with Axial‐to‐Central Chirality Conversion
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 17, 5 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
Atropisomers are exciting synthetic targets with unique properties, which find applications in many fields of chemistry. However, considering them as substrates is not very frequent, notably in the context of dearomatization reaction. We wish to discuss the different strategies for the dearomatization of aromatic atropisomers with axial‐to‐central chirality conversion to afford high‐value compounds that are difficult to access via other synthetic routes.
Aromatic atropisomers, that is to say aromatic compounds that exhibit chirality because of a hindered rotation around the bond linking them to one of their substituents, have recently received an increased interest, both as synthetic targets and as molecules of interest for applications as catalysts or in medicinal chemistry. However, despite the easier access to diversified enantioenriched aromatic atropisomers, considering them as substrates is not very frequent, notably in the context of dearomatization reactions. It is even more regrettable, as such transformations can deliver enantioenriched original motifs through an axial‐to‐central chirality conversion. The goal of this review is to provide a general overview of dearomative transformations of aromatic atropisomers to help the synthetic chemists community identifying new potential research directions in this subfield.
item.content:encoded:
Atropisomers are exciting synthetic targets with unique properties, which find applications in many fields of chemistry. However, considering them as substrates is not very frequent, notably in the context of dearomatization reaction. We wish to discuss the different strategies for the dearomatization of aromatic atropisomers with axial-to-central chirality conversion to afford high-value compounds that are difficult to access via other synthetic routes.


Aromatic atropisomers, that is to say aromatic compounds that exhibit chirality because of a hindered rotation around the bond linking them to one of their substituents, have recently received an increased interest, both as synthetic targets and as molecules of interest for applications as catalysts or in medicinal chemistry. However, despite the easier access to diversified enantioenriched aromatic atropisomers, considering them as substrates is not very frequent, notably in the context of dearomatization reactions. It is even more regrettable, as such transformations can deliver enantioenriched original motifs through an axial-to-central chirality conversion. The goal of this review is to provide a general overview of dearomative transformations of aromatic atropisomers to help the synthetic chemists community identifying new potential research directions in this subfield.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Furo‐/Thieno[2,3‐c]pyridine Derivatives: Synthesis, Reactions, and Applications
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 17, 5 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
Furo‐/thienopyridines, as fused heterocyclic pyridine derivatives, have attracted increasing interest in modern organic chemistry due to a growing number of pharmaceutical and material science studies. Accordingly, substantial progress has been achieved in the development of efficient and versatile synthetic methodologies for their construction. This review provides a comprehensive overview of the synthetic strategies for furo‐/thieno[2,3‐c]pyridines and their derivatives, while also highlighting their fields of application.
Furo‐/thienopyridines constitute a class of heterocyclic compounds that have garnered significant attention in medicinal chemistry due to their diverse biological activities and potential therapeutic applications. These compounds represent a versatile class that, in addition to their importance in medicinal chemistry, also have significant implications in materials science. Ongoing research into their synthesis and functionalization is essential for unlocking their full potential. This review features synthetic approaches to the preparation of furo‐/thienopyridines published from 2008 until 2025, with a primary focus on the [2,3‐c] fusion. Older reports will be mentioned where appropriate for comparison. The characteristics and applications of the described compounds are discussed and summarized in tables.
item.content:encoded:
Furo-/thienopyridines, as fused heterocyclic pyridine derivatives, have attracted increasing interest in modern organic chemistry due to a growing number of pharmaceutical and material science studies. Accordingly, substantial progress has been achieved in the development of efficient and versatile synthetic methodologies for their construction. This review provides a comprehensive overview of the synthetic strategies for furo-/thieno[2,3-c]pyridines and their derivatives, while also highlighting their fields of application.


Furo-/thienopyridines constitute a class of heterocyclic compounds that have garnered significant attention in medicinal chemistry due to their diverse biological activities and potential therapeutic applications. These compounds represent a versatile class that, in addition to their importance in medicinal chemistry, also have significant implications in materials science. Ongoing research into their synthesis and functionalization is essential for unlocking their full potential. This review features synthetic approaches to the preparation of furo-/thienopyridines published from 2008 until 2025, with a primary focus on the [2,3-c] fusion. Older reports will be mentioned where appropriate for comparison. The characteristics and applications of the described compounds are discussed and summarized in tables.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: QuinoxP*: A Privileged Ligand in Asymmetric Transition‐Metal Catalysis
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 17, 5 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
This review provides a comprehensive overview of the development and applications of the P‐stereogenic ligand QuinoxP*, covering a wide range of transition‐metal‐catalyzed reactions (Rh, Cu, Pd, Ni, Co, Ir, Ag, and Fe), thus demonstrating the exceptionally strong chiral control ability of QuinoxP* and related ligands.
QuinoxP*, characterized by a quinoxaline backbone bearing two P‐stereogenic phosphine units, demonstrates excellent activity and stereoselectivity in asymmetric transition‐metal catalysis. Since its initial synthesis in 2005, and especially over the past decade, there has been a notable increase in studies reporting its application in transition‐metal‐catalyzed asymmetric transformations. Despite this expanding body of literature, a comprehensive review dedicated to this privileged ligand remains absent. To address this gap, the present review provides a thorough overview of the origin of QuinoxP* and its structural features and key properties, as well as its diverse catalytic applications in the past decade. Furthermore, we examine its current limitations and discuss future directions for the advancement and utilization of this remarkable ligand.
item.content:encoded:
This review provides a comprehensive overview of the development and applications of the P-stereogenic ligand QuinoxP*, covering a wide range of transition-metal-catalyzed reactions (Rh, Cu, Pd, Ni, Co, Ir, Ag, and Fe), thus demonstrating the exceptionally strong chiral control ability of QuinoxP* and related ligands.


QuinoxP*, characterized by a quinoxaline backbone bearing two P-stereogenic phosphine units, demonstrates excellent activity and stereoselectivity in asymmetric transition-metal catalysis. Since its initial synthesis in 2005, and especially over the past decade, there has been a notable increase in studies reporting its application in transition-metal-catalyzed asymmetric transformations. Despite this expanding body of literature, a comprehensive review dedicated to this privileged ligand remains absent. To address this gap, the present review provides a thorough overview of the origin of QuinoxP* and its structural features and key properties, as well as its diverse catalytic applications in the past decade. Furthermore, we examine its current limitations and discuss future directions for the advancement and utilization of this remarkable ligand.
item.dc:creator: Jinbao Wang, Yuyang Liao, Chun Wang, Yumei Xiao, Cheng Zhang
item.dc:title: QuinoxP*: A Privileged Ligand in Asymmetric Transition‐Metal Catalysis

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Isatogens and Their Reactivity Landscape
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, Volume 29, Issue 17, 5 May 2026.
item.dc:description:
Isatogens (3H‐indol‐3‐one N‐oxides) are compact yet highly versatile heterocycles whose embedded N–O functionality imparts distinctive redox behavior and enables skeletal reorganization. Originally encountered as oxidation products in indigo chemistry, isatogens have since evolved into valuable synthetic platforms, granting access to 3‐indolones, pseudoindoxyls, and structurally remodeled heterocycles. This Review surveys their properties, pharmacology, preparation, and modern synthetic applications.
Isatogen (indol‐3‐one N‐oxide) has attracted renewed attention in synthetic chemistry in recent years. First discovered by Adolf von Baeyer in 1881–1882, its potential as a bioactive scaffold and as a versatile synthetic intermediate remained sporadically explored for much of its history. The recent expansion of the literature has highlighted the distinctive features of the isatogen framework, notably the oxidized nitrogen center embedded within an aromatic system, which confer unusual and exploitable reactivity. This Review provides an up‐to‐date and comprehensive account of isatogen chemistry, encompassing both methods for its preparation and its synthetic applications. Recent advances are integrated with earlier work to present a coherent picture of isatogen reactivity across electrophilic activation, redox processes, cyclizations, and functionalization reactions. The Review is organized to cover historical background, reported bioactivities, and synthetic chemistry, with emphasis on preparative strategies and downstream transformations. By consolidating and critically assessing contemporary developments, this Review clarifies established reactivity patterns, underscores their practical relevance, and aims to stimulate further exploration of isatogens as dynamic scaffolds for modern synthetic strategy development.
item.content:encoded:
Isatogens (3H-indol-3-one N-oxides) are compact yet highly versatile heterocycles whose embedded N–O functionality imparts distinctive redox behavior and enables skeletal reorganization. Originally encountered as oxidation products in indigo chemistry, isatogens have since evolved into valuable synthetic platforms, granting access to 3-indolones, pseudoindoxyls, and structurally remodeled heterocycles. This Review surveys their properties, pharmacology, preparation, and modern synthetic applications.


Isatogen (indol-3-one N-oxide) has attracted renewed attention in synthetic chemistry in recent years. First discovered by Adolf von Baeyer in 1881–1882, its potential as a bioactive scaffold and as a versatile synthetic intermediate remained sporadically explored for much of its history. The recent expansion of the literature has highlighted the distinctive features of the isatogen framework, notably the oxidized nitrogen center embedded within an aromatic system, which confer unusual and exploitable reactivity. This Review provides an up-to-date and comprehensive account of isatogen chemistry, encompassing both methods for its preparation and its synthetic applications. Recent advances are integrated with earlier work to present a coherent picture of isatogen reactivity across electrophilic activation, redox processes, cyclizations, and functionalization reactions. The Review is organized to cover historical background, reported bioactivities, and synthetic chemistry, with emphasis on preparative strategies and downstream transformations. By consolidating and critically assessing contemporary developments, this Review clarifies established reactivity patterns, underscores their practical relevance, and aims to stimulate further exploration of isatogens as dynamic scaffolds for modern synthetic strategy development.
item.dc:creator: Jumreang Tummatorn, Somsak Ruchirawat, Charnsak Thongsornkleeb
item.dc:title: Isatogens and Their Reactivity Landscape

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Peptide Bond Formation via Anhydride Exchange‐Mediated In Situ Generation of N‐Methylimidazolium Cation
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
Herein, we report a novel peptide bond formation strategy relying on anhydride exchange, which enables the in situ generation of N‐methylimidazolium cations by using trimethylacetic anhydride and catalytic N‐methylimidazole.
Herein, we report a method for peptide bond formation via anhydride exchange‐mediated in situ generation of N‐methylimidazolium cations using trimethylacetic anhydride (Piv2O) and catalytic N‐methylimidazole (NMI). It tolerates diverse amino acid side chains (e.g., −OH, indole NH) with no detectable racemization/epimerization even when dipeptides serve as electrophiles. Convergent synthesis of protected Leu‐enkephalin underscores its utility, expanding the toolbox for peptide bond formation.
item.content:encoded:
Herein, we report a novel peptide bond formation strategy relying on anhydride exchange, which enables the in situ generation of N-methylimidazolium cations by using trimethylacetic anhydride and catalytic N-methylimidazole.


Herein, we report a method for peptide bond formation via anhydride exchange-mediated in situ generation of N-methylimidazolium cations using trimethylacetic anhydride (Piv2O) and catalytic N-methylimidazole (NMI). It tolerates diverse amino acid side chains (e.g., −OH, indole NH) with no detectable racemization/epimerization even when dipeptides serve as electrophiles. Convergent synthesis of protected Leu-enkephalin underscores its utility, expanding the toolbox for peptide bond formation.
item.dc:creator: Chenchen Qi, Lulu Liu, Xiangsheng Xu, Xiaoqing Li
item.dc:title: Peptide Bond Formation via Anhydride Exchange‐Mediated In Situ Generation of N‐Methylimidazolium Cation

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Sustainable Synthesis of 2‐(Arylamino)nicotinic Acids Using Concentrated Solar Radiation
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A sustainable synthesis of 2‐(arylamino)nicotinic acids is achieved using concentrated solar radiation under catalyst‐ and solvent‐free conditions. This green method provides excellent yields of pharmaceutically relevant compounds in minutes. The protocol is successfully demonstrated on a gram scale for drugs like flunixin and clonixin, offering a rapid, energy‐efficient, and environmentally benign alternative to conventional synthetic routes.
2‐(Arylamino)nicotinic acids are significant in synthetic chemistry, particularly due to their applications in the pharmaceutical industry. An environmentally sustainable methodology has been developed for synthesizing these compounds, employing concentrated solar radiation (CSR) as a renewable energy source under catalyst‐ and solvent‐free conditions. This approach facilitates the rapid and selective synthesis of these biologically important compounds, achieving yields of up to 94% under mild conditions. Moreover, this method's synthetic applicability is demonstrated through synthesizing pharmaceutical compounds such as flunixin and clonixin on a gram scale. This eco‐friendly approach offers a promising alternative to traditional synthesis techniques and emphasizes the potential of CSR in promoting sustainable chemical processes.
item.content:encoded:
A sustainable synthesis of 2-(arylamino)nicotinic acids is achieved using concentrated solar radiation under catalyst- and solvent-free conditions. This green method provides excellent yields of pharmaceutically relevant compounds in minutes. The protocol is successfully demonstrated on a gram scale for drugs like flunixin and clonixin, offering a rapid, energy-efficient, and environmentally benign alternative to conventional synthetic routes.


2-(Arylamino)nicotinic acids are significant in synthetic chemistry, particularly due to their applications in the pharmaceutical industry. An environmentally sustainable methodology has been developed for synthesizing these compounds, employing concentrated solar radiation (CSR) as a renewable energy source under catalyst- and solvent-free conditions. This approach facilitates the rapid and selective synthesis of these biologically important compounds, achieving yields of up to 94% under mild conditions. Moreover, this method's synthetic applicability is demonstrated through synthesizing pharmaceutical compounds such as flunixin and clonixin on a gram scale. This eco-friendly approach offers a promising alternative to traditional synthesis techniques and emphasizes the potential of CSR in promoting sustainable chemical processes.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Mechanochemical Hydrodeoxygenations
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A silver‐catalyzed mechanochemical hydrodeoxygenation with triethylsilane as reductant allows to convert ketones to the corresponding methylene derivatives in good yields after a short reaction time. Selected examples show that also alcohols, ethers, and sulfoxides can react.
In this work, a mechanochemical hydrodeoxygenation (HDO) was developed. Using silver triflate as catalyst and triethylsilane as reducing agent, 18 ketones and other substrates were hydrodeoxygenated in yields up to 92%.

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Rh(III)‐Catalyzed [4+2] Annulation of Imidazoles With α‐Diazocarbonyl Compounds: Direct Access to π‐Conjugated Imidazole‐Fused Isoquinolinone
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A Rh(III)‐catalyzed [4 + 2] annulation of 2‐aryl imidazoles/benzimidazoles with α‐diazocarbonyl compounds is developed for the synthesis of π‐conjugated imidazo/benzimidazo[2,1‐a]isoquinolinones systems. Mechanistic studies and control experiment reveal that the reaction proceeds via imidazole‐directed ortho C(sp2)–H activation, followed by carbene C–H insertion and subsequent intramolecular amidation to furnish the [4 + 2] annulation product.
A Rh(III)‐catalyzed [4 + 2] annulation of 2‐aryl imidazoles/benzimidazoles with α‐diazocarbonyl compounds has been developed for the synthesis of π‐conjugated imidazo/benzimidazo[2,1‐a]isoquinolinones systems. This method exhibits a broad substrate scope and excellent functional group tolerance, enabling the access of diverse tricyclic and tetracyclic π‐extended imidazole and benzimidazole derivatives. Mechanistic studies and control experiment revealed that the reaction proceeds via imidazole‐directed ortho C(sp2)–H activation, followed by carbene C–H insertion and subsequent intramolecular amidation to furnish the [4 + 2] annulation product. An optimized scaffold demonstrated potential relevance in materials and medicinal chemistry.
item.content:encoded:
A Rh(III)-catalyzed [4 + 2] annulation of 2-aryl imidazoles/benzimidazoles with α-diazocarbonyl compounds is developed for the synthesis of π-conjugated imidazo/benzimidazo[2,1-a]isoquinolinones systems. Mechanistic studies and control experiment reveal that the reaction proceeds via imidazole-directed ortho C(sp2)–H activation, followed by carbene C–H insertion and subsequent intramolecular amidation to furnish the [4 + 2] annulation product.


A Rh(III)-catalyzed [4 + 2] annulation of 2-aryl imidazoles/benzimidazoles with α-diazocarbonyl compounds has been developed for the synthesis of π-conjugated imidazo/benzimidazo[2,1-a]isoquinolinones systems. This method exhibits a broad substrate scope and excellent functional group tolerance, enabling the access of diverse tricyclic and tetracyclic π-extended imidazole and benzimidazole derivatives. Mechanistic studies and control experiment revealed that the reaction proceeds via imidazole-directed ortho C(sp2)–H activation, followed by carbene C–H insertion and subsequent intramolecular amidation to furnish the [4 + 2] annulation product. An optimized scaffold demonstrated potential relevance in materials and medicinal chemistry.
item.dc:creator: Ganesh P. Pawar, Duc Huy Le, Li Ching Shen, Sheng‐Cih Huang, Chung‐Ming Sun
item.dc:title: Rh(III)‐Catalyzed [4+2] Annulation of Imidazoles With α‐Diazocarbonyl Compounds: Direct Access to π‐Conjugated Imidazole‐Fused Isoquinolinone

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Pd‐Catalyzed Cyclotrimerization and Cycloaddition of 5,6‐Didehydrobenzo[8]annulenes (BenzoCOTynes): Formation of Nonplanar Tri[8]annulenes and Diels–Alder Cycloadducts
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A Pd(0)‐catalyzed cyclotrimerization of benzoCOTynes to form tri(benzo[8]annulenes) was developed. Three distinct isomers were isolated and structurally characterized by single‐crystal X‐ray diffraction. Ligand modulation proved crucial for enhancing regioselectivity: P(4‐OMePh), and (S)‐tBu‐Phox promoted the formation of the symmetric isomers. Both benzoCOTyne and benzodiCOTyne readily participated in Diels–Alder cycloadditions, affording the corresponding adducts in good yields.
We investigated the Pd(0)‐catalyzed cyclotrimerization of benzoCOTynes, which afforded a set of regioisomeric tri(benzo[8]annulene) products. Three distinct isomers were isolated and unambiguously characterized by single‐crystal X‐ray diffraction. Ligand modulation proved crucial for enhancing regioselectivity, with P(4‐OMePh)3 and (S)tBu‐Phox promoting the formation of the symmetric isomers. The resulting polycyclic frameworks exhibited high thermal stability, remaining intact up to 140°C. Furthermore, both benzoCOTyne and benzodiCOTyne readily participated in Diels–Alder cycloadditions, delivering the corresponding adducts in good yields.
item.content:encoded:
A Pd(0)-catalyzed cyclotrimerization of benzoCOTynes to form tri(benzo[8]annulenes) was developed. Three distinct isomers were isolated and structurally characterized by single-crystal X-ray diffraction. Ligand modulation proved crucial for enhancing regioselectivity: P(4-OMePh), and (S)-tBu-Phox promoted the formation of the symmetric isomers. Both benzoCOTyne and benzodiCOTyne readily participated in Diels–Alder cycloadditions, affording the corresponding adducts in good yields.


We investigated the Pd(0)-catalyzed cyclotrimerization of benzoCOTynes, which afforded a set of regioisomeric tri(benzo[8]annulene) products. Three distinct isomers were isolated and unambiguously characterized by single-crystal X-ray diffraction. Ligand modulation proved crucial for enhancing regioselectivity, with P(4-OMePh)3 and (S)tBu-Phox promoting the formation of the symmetric isomers. The resulting polycyclic frameworks exhibited high thermal stability, remaining intact up to 140°C. Furthermore, both benzoCOTyne and benzodiCOTyne readily participated in Diels–Alder cycloadditions, delivering the corresponding adducts in good yields.
item.dc:creator: Jesús Bello‐García, Jesús A. Varela, Carlos Saá
item.dc:title: Pd‐Catalyzed Cyclotrimerization and Cycloaddition of 5,6‐Didehydrobenzo[8]annulenes (BenzoCOTynes): Formation of Nonplanar Tri[8]annulenes and Diels–Alder Cycloadducts

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Advances in Synthesis and Applications of Azoxybenzenes
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
The synthesis approaches to access azoxybenzenes employing thermal, photo‐redox mechanical and electrochemical conditions have been discussed. Their applications in key reactions including nitrene transfer reactions, azoxy group transfer, heterocyclic synthesis, and as directing groups in catalysis have been explored describing the recent trends and mechanistic interventions.
Azoxybenzene derivatives are important scaffolds in organic chemistry. These are widely used molecules having diverse bioactivity and several applications including fine chemicals synthesis, heterocyclic scaffolds synthesis, as directing groups in functionalization reactions and use as organic dyes. These are the vital intermediates observed during the redox pathways for the nitrobenzene to aniline conversions and vice‐versa. It is quite challenging to extract these intermediates as the final product from these redox pathways. Herein, we have focused on the synthesis approaches to access azoxybenzenes employing thermal, photo‐redox mechanical, and electrochemical conditions. Additionally, their applications in key reactions including nitrene transfer reactions, azoxy group transfer, heterocyclic synthesis, and as directing groups in catalysis have been explored describing the recent trends and mechanistic interventions.
item.content:encoded:
The synthesis approaches to access azoxybenzenes employing thermal, photo-redox mechanical and electrochemical conditions have been discussed. Their applications in key reactions including nitrene transfer reactions, azoxy group transfer, heterocyclic synthesis, and as directing groups in catalysis have been explored describing the recent trends and mechanistic interventions.


Azoxybenzene derivatives are important scaffolds in organic chemistry. These are widely used molecules having diverse bioactivity and several applications including fine chemicals synthesis, heterocyclic scaffolds synthesis, as directing groups in functionalization reactions and use as organic dyes. These are the vital intermediates observed during the redox pathways for the nitrobenzene to aniline conversions and vice-versa. It is quite challenging to extract these intermediates as the final product from these redox pathways. Herein, we have focused on the synthesis approaches to access azoxybenzenes employing thermal, photo-redox mechanical, and electrochemical conditions. Additionally, their applications in key reactions including nitrene transfer reactions, azoxy group transfer, heterocyclic synthesis, and as directing groups in catalysis have been explored describing the recent trends and mechanistic interventions.
item.dc:creator: Sandeep Kumar, Avinash Kumar, Aviti Sakshith, Ankush Gupta, Praveen Kumar Verma
item.dc:title: Recent Advances in Synthesis and Applications of Azoxybenzenes

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Metal‐Free [4‐OH‐TEMPO]+[NO3]−‐Catalyzed Aerobic Oxidation of Benzylic C(sp3)–H Bonds in Ethers
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A cheap organocatalyst, [4‐OH‐TEMPO]+[NO3]−, catalyzes the aerobic oxidation of benzylic C(sp3)H bonds in ethers under mild conditions. Hydride abstraction by the oxoammonium cation generates a stabilized carbocation intermediate, which undergoes nucleophilic attack by water to form the product. The hydroxylamine is regenerated to the oxoammonium cation via a NOx/O2 cycle.
A cheap organocatalyst, [4‐OH‐TEMPO]+[NO3]−, is presented for the aerobic oxidation of benzylic C(sp3)H bonds in ethers. The catalytic efficiency is attributed to the cooperative action of both the cation and anion moieties. The oxoammonium cation facilitates hydride abstraction from the C(sp3)H bond, generating a stabilized carbocation intermediate, which is subsequently attacked by water to form the CO bond. Meanwhile, the nitrate anion participates in a NOx/O2 cycle, regenerating the oxoammonium cation from its reduced hydroxylamine form. Under mild reaction conditions, this salt catalyzes the oxidation of ethers to produce lactones or ketones in excellent yields, even on a 10 mmol scale. Control experiments confirm that the carbocation intermediate is essential to the reaction and that the oxygen atom in the product is derived from water.
item.content:encoded:
A cheap organocatalyst, [4-OH-TEMPO]+[NO3]−, catalyzes the aerobic oxidation of benzylic C(sp3)H bonds in ethers under mild conditions. Hydride abstraction by the oxoammonium cation generates a stabilized carbocation intermediate, which undergoes nucleophilic attack by water to form the product. The hydroxylamine is regenerated to the oxoammonium cation via a NO x /O2 cycle.


A cheap organocatalyst, [4-OH-TEMPO]+[NO3]−, is presented for the aerobic oxidation of benzylic C(sp3)H bonds in ethers. The catalytic efficiency is attributed to the cooperative action of both the cation and anion moieties. The oxoammonium cation facilitates hydride abstraction from the C(sp3)H bond, generating a stabilized carbocation intermediate, which is subsequently attacked by water to form the CO bond. Meanwhile, the nitrate anion participates in a NO x /O2 cycle, regenerating the oxoammonium cation from its reduced hydroxylamine form. Under mild reaction conditions, this salt catalyzes the oxidation of ethers to produce lactones or ketones in excellent yields, even on a 10 mmol scale. Control experiments confirm that the carbocation intermediate is essential to the reaction and that the oxygen atom in the product is derived from water.
item.dc:creator: Junpeng Chen, Yongtao Wang, Wanqi Sun, Jia Yao, Haoran Li
item.dc:title: Metal‐Free [4‐OH‐TEMPO]+[NO3]−‐Catalyzed Aerobic Oxidation of Benzylic C(sp3)–H Bonds in Ethers

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Electrochemical Borylation of Aryldiazonium Salts: A Catalyst‐Free Approach to CB Bond Formation
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
A novel electrochemical method enables the synthesis of arylboronates from aryldiazonium salts and B2pin2 in an undivided cell under constant‐current electrolysis, using electricity as the sole redox agent. The metal‐ and additive‐free protocol exhibits broad substrate scope and delivers products in moderate to good yields, with most cases exceeding 70%.
This study presents an electrochemical strategy for the synthesis of arylboronates from aryldiazonium salts and bis(pinacolato)diboron (B2pin2). The transformation is achieved via constant‐current electrolysis in an undivided cell, without requiring external oxidants or reductants, metal catalysts, acid/base additives, or any additional promoters. Notably, the method exhibits excellent functional‐group tolerance, accommodating a wide range of structurally diverse substrates and delivering the target arylboronates in moderate to good yields, with most exceeding 70%.
item.content:encoded:
A novel electrochemical method enables the synthesis of arylboronates from aryldiazonium salts and B2pin2 in an undivided cell under constant-current electrolysis, using electricity as the sole redox agent. The metal- and additive-free protocol exhibits broad substrate scope and delivers products in moderate to good yields, with most cases exceeding 70%.


This study presents an electrochemical strategy for the synthesis of arylboronates from aryldiazonium salts and bis(pinacolato)diboron (B2pin2). The transformation is achieved via constant-current electrolysis in an undivided cell, without requiring external oxidants or reductants, metal catalysts, acid/base additives, or any additional promoters. Notably, the method exhibits excellent functional-group tolerance, accommodating a wide range of structurally diverse substrates and delivering the target arylboronates in moderate to good yields, with most exceeding 70%.
item.dc:creator: Jie Zhang, Xin Guan, Wenjie Zhang, Zeizhao Huang, Zeyao Liang, Li Zhu, Xiaoquan Yao
item.dc:title: Electrochemical Borylation of Aryldiazonium Salts: A Catalyst‐Free Approach to CB Bond Formation

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: A Convenient Undivided Cell Strategy for the Electrochemical Gewald Reaction
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
In this work, we introduce a practical electrochemical Gewald protocol in a commercial, undivided cell reactor. Our optimized conditions enable the efficient synthesis of functionalized 2‐aminothiophenes, which are privileged scaffolds in medicinal chemistry, while accommodating complex substrates and drug molecules under mild, reproducible conditions.
The Gewald reaction is a key method for synthesizing 2‐aminothiophenes, which are valuable scaffolds in drug discovery. Here, we report a practical electrochemical approach to the Gewald reaction using an undivided cell setup in the IKA ElectraSyn 2.0 reactor. Key parameters, including electrode materials, electrolyte type, current, and electron equivalents, were systematically investigated to maximize yields. The optimized protocol demonstrated broad substrate scope, tolerating diverse functional groups and ring systems. Late‐stage functionalization and a one‐pot transformation were achieved on complex molecules, and the method was successfully applied to the synthesis of the non‐steroidal anti‐inflammatory drug tinoridine. This robust, scalable, and sustainable electrochemical strategy offers a convenient and complementary method to traditional Gewald reactions, expanding access to 2‐aminothiophenes for medicinal chemistry.
item.content:encoded:
In this work, we introduce a practical electrochemical Gewald protocol in a commercial, undivided cell reactor. Our optimized conditions enable the efficient synthesis of functionalized 2-aminothiophenes, which are privileged scaffolds in medicinal chemistry, while accommodating complex substrates and drug molecules under mild, reproducible conditions.


The Gewald reaction is a key method for synthesizing 2-aminothiophenes, which are valuable scaffolds in drug discovery. Here, we report a practical electrochemical approach to the Gewald reaction using an undivided cell setup in the IKA ElectraSyn 2.0 reactor. Key parameters, including electrode materials, electrolyte type, current, and electron equivalents, were systematically investigated to maximize yields. The optimized protocol demonstrated broad substrate scope, tolerating diverse functional groups and ring systems. Late-stage functionalization and a one-pot transformation were achieved on complex molecules, and the method was successfully applied to the synthesis of the non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug tinoridine. This robust, scalable, and sustainable electrochemical strategy offers a convenient and complementary method to traditional Gewald reactions, expanding access to 2-aminothiophenes for medicinal chemistry.

item.dc:title: A Convenient Undivided Cell Strategy for the Electrochemical Gewald Reaction

item.source: Wiley: European Journal of Organic Chemistry: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Advances in [5 + 4] Cycloaddition Reactions: Catalysis, Mechanism, and Reaction Optimization
item.description: European Journal of Organic Chemistry, EarlyView.
item.dc:description:
[5+4] cycloaddition emerges as a powerful yet underexplored strategy for constructing challenging nine‐membered rings. This review highlights advances from 2000 to 2025, showcasing how metal, organo‐, and photocatalysts overcome thermodynamic and transannular barriers, enabling efficient synthesis of medium‐sized scaffolds with significant biological relevance.
Medium‐sized rings, especially nine‐membered scaffolds, represent the core framework of several promising biologically active compounds. Among the various synthetic strategies reported, cycloaddition reactions, particularly [5 + 4] cycloaddition reactions, represent a unique and relatively underexplored class of pericyclic reactions that form the nine‐membered ring systems. However, the construction of a nine‐membered ring remains a significant challenge due to thermodynamic barriers, involving unfavorable enthalpic and entropic factors, as well as transannular interactions. Numerous catalytic systems have been developed to overcome these challenges. In this review article, we examined literature from 2000 to 2025 on [5 + 4] cycloaddition reaction, highlighting the role of various catalytic strategies such as metal catalysts, organocatalysts, and photocatalysts.
item.content:encoded:
[5+4] cycloaddition emerges as a powerful yet underexplored strategy for constructing challenging nine-membered rings. This review highlights advances from 2000 to 2025, showcasing how metal, organo-, and photocatalysts overcome thermodynamic and transannular barriers, enabling efficient synthesis of medium-sized scaffolds with significant biological relevance.


Medium-sized rings, especially nine-membered scaffolds, represent the core framework of several promising biologically active compounds. Among the various synthetic strategies reported, cycloaddition reactions, particularly [5 + 4] cycloaddition reactions, represent a unique and relatively underexplored class of pericyclic reactions that form the nine-membered ring systems. However, the construction of a nine-membered ring remains a significant challenge due to thermodynamic barriers, involving unfavorable enthalpic and entropic factors, as well as transannular interactions. Numerous catalytic systems have been developed to overcome these challenges. In this review article, we examined literature from 2000 to 2025 on [5 + 4] cycloaddition reaction, highlighting the role of various catalytic strategies such as metal catalysts, organocatalysts, and photocatalysts.
